perturbation.original_text: Agent shall not be liable for any failures, delays or losses, arising directly or indirectly out of conditions beyond its reasonable control, including, but not limited to, acts of government, exchange or market ruling, suspension of trading, work stoppages or labor disputes, civil disobedience, riots, rebellions, electrical or mechanical failure, computer hardware or software failure, communications facilities failures including telephone failure, war, terrorism, insurrection, fires, earthquakes, storms, floods, acts of God or similar occurrences.
perturbation.changed_text: Agent shall not be liable for any failures, delays or losses arising directly out of conditions beyond its reasonable control.
perturbation.explanation: Removing specific examples of 'force majeure' conditions could be problematic. New York law requires contracts to be specific in defining and limiting force majeure clauses, particularly related to foreseeable disruptions. This omission creates ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The altered text removes specific protections afforded by force majeure clauses.  Under N.Y. U.C.C. § 2-615, a seller (here, Agent providing services) is excused from performance when performance has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made.  By omitting specific examples (like government acts or system failures), the contract becomes vague and limits the Agent's liability less clearly, potentially violating the spirit of the UCC requiring specificity in unforeseen circumstances that excuse performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 6 Breach, Repudiation and Excuse previous SECTION 2-614 Substituted Performance up PART 6 Breach, Repudiation and Excuse next SECTION 2-616 Procedure on Notice Claiming Excuse This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-615 Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 6 Section 2--615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. Except so far as a seller may have assumed a greater obligation and subject to the preceding section on substituted performance: (a) Delay in delivery or non-delivery in whole or in part by a seller who complies with paragraphs (b) and (c) is not a breach of his duty under a contract for sale if performance as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made or by compliance in good faith with any applicable foreign or domestic governmental regulation or order whether or not it later proves to be invalid. (b) Where the causes mentioned in paragraph (a) affect only a part of the seller's capacity to perform, he must allocate production and deliveries among his customers but may at his option include regular customers not then under contract as well as his own requirements for further manufacture. He may so allocate in any manner which is fair and reasonable. (c) The seller must notify the buyer seasonably that there will be delay or non-delivery and, when allocation is required under paragraph (b), of the estimated quota thus made available for the buyer.
perturbation.justification: The altered text removes specific examples of 'force majeure' conditions. The omission weakens the protection afforded to the Agent. N.Y. U.C.C. § 2-615, as stated in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, excuses a seller from performance when performance has been made impracticable by a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption. By removing specific examples like government acts or system failures, the contract becomes vague and limits the Agent's liability less clearly, potentially violating the spirit of the UCC requiring specificity in unforeseen circumstances that excuse performance. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: In no event will Agent be liable for special, indirect, incidental, consequential or punitive losses or damages of any kind whatsoever (including but not limited to lost profits), even if Agent has been advised of the possibility of such losses or damages and regardless of the form of action. Any liability of Agent will be limited in the aggregate to an amount equal to twenty four (24) times the monthly administrative fee to be paid by Client as set forth in Exhibit B hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: In no event will Agent be liable for punitive losses or damages of any kind whatsoever, regardless of the form of action.
perturbation.explanation: Excluding liability for 'special, indirect, incidental, consequential losses' while retaining a limitation based on a multiple of monthly fees could be challenged. The lack of clarity on foreseeable direct damages creates ambiguity. While limitation of liability clauses are generally enforceable, New York courts scrutinize them, especially if they are unconscionable or violate public policy, or if direct damages are not clearly addressed.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CLS UCC § 2-719 governs contractual modification or limitation of remedy.  While it permits limiting or excluding consequential damages, it states that such limitations are not enforceable if they are unconscionable. By broadly eliminating liability for many types of direct losses and damages, the revised text potentially creates a situation where the client has no meaningful remedy for a breach, making the limitation potentially unconscionable, particularly if foreseeable direct damages are excluded without specific alternative remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 7 Remedies previous SECTION 2-718 Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits up PART 7 Remedies next SECTION 2-720 Effect of "Cancellation" or "Rescission" on Claims for Antecedent Breach This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-719 Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 7 Section 2--719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1) Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text omits liability for 'special, indirect, incidental, consequential losses'. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest this omission, when combined with the retention of a limitation based on monthly fees, creates a potential situation where the client has no meaningful remedy for a breach, making the limitation potentially unconscionable under NY CLS UCC § 2-719. The scraped law snippet, specifically UCC 2-719(3), states that 'Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable.' The omission of various damage types, without addressing foreseeable direct damages, arguably leads to such an unconscionable outcome, creating a legal contradiction according to the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Client acknowledges that Agent is subject to the customer identification program ("Customer Identification Program") requirements under the USA PATRIOT Act and its implementing regulations, and that Agent must obtain, verify and record information that allows Agent to identify Client. Accordingly, prior to accepting an appointment hereunder, Agent may request information from Client that will help Agent to identify Client, including without limitation Client's physical address, tax identification number, organizational documents, certificate of good standing, license to do business, or any other information that Agent deems necessary. Client agrees that Agent cannot accept an appointment hereunder unless and until Agent verifies Client's identity in accordance with the Customer Identification Program requirements.
perturbation.changed_text: Client acknowledges that Agent is subject to the customer identification program requirements. Agent may request information from Client that will help Agent to identify Client.
perturbation.explanation: Omitting the specific reference to the USA PATRIOT Act and the Agent's explicit obligation to verify and record client information weakens the clause. While the basic acknowledgement remains, the omission of these details obscures the legal mandate and reduces transparency about the Agent's obligations and the Client's rights. This creates a potential ambiguity, as it suggests the CIP is discretionary rather than legally required.
perturbation.law_explanation: 31 U.S.C. § 5318(l) mandates the establishment of Customer Identification Programs (CIPs) by financial institutions. The modified text's removal of specific requirements to verify and record information contradicts the explicit obligations imposed by the USA PATRIOT Act and its implementing regulations. It suggests that CIP implementation is at the agent's discretion rather than a legal mandate. This weakens the contractual acknowledgment of compliance with federal law.

Compliance, exemptions, and summons authority (a) General Powers of Secretary .-The Secretary of the Treasury may (except under section 5315 of this title and regulations prescribed under section 5315)- (1) except as provided in subsections (b)(2) and (h)(4), delegate duti
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly references the USA PATRIOT Act and the agent's obligation to verify and record client information as part of the Customer Identification Program (CIP). The changed text omits these details. The Law Explanation states that 31 U.S.C. § 5318(l) mandates the establishment of CIPs by financial institutions, and that removing specific requirements to verify and record information contradicts the explicit obligations imposed by the USA PATRIOT Act and its implementing regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 31 USC 5318, which supports that the original contract was compliant but the changed one is not.
